claim: The Currier A and Currier B sub-corpora use two structurally distinct homophonic cipher tables encoding the same underlying Latin text: Currier A uses a wider homophone set per plaintext letter (lower per-glyph entropy ~3.75 bits) while Currier B uses a narrower set (higher per-glyph entropy ~3.90 bits), producing the observed entropy difference between the two dialects
evidence_for: Multiple high-confidence existing hypotheses (0.977â€“0.982) support Currier A/B as two distinct cipher tables over the same underlying language
Currier B is larger (23,766 words vs 11,022) and if it uses a narrower homophone set its entropy should be measurably higher
Section-level entropy variation (3.71â€“3.90 bits) is consistent with varying homophone ratios per section, not with a single fixed cipher
Glyph inventory of 25 is consistent with a homophonic expansion of a Latin alphabet of ~23 letters
evidence_against: Underlying language may differ between scribes rather than cipher table alone
If both scribes use identical homophone ratios, per-sub-corpus entropy difference would be negligible
Word-level entropy (10.45 bits) may swamp glyph-level entropy differences between A and B
test: Partition corpus into Currier A (11,022 words) and Currier B (23,766 words) tokens. Compute per-glyph Shannon entropy separately for each sub-corpus. Compute bigram glyph entropy and type-token ratio per sub-corpus. Test whether the entropy difference is statistically significant via bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations).
test_metric: Per-glyph entropy difference H(B) - H(A), bigram entropy per sub-corpus, bootstrap 95% CI on entropy difference
test_threshold: Confirmation: H(B) - H(A) > 0.05 bits with bootstrap p < 0.05. Denial: |H(B) - H(A)| < 0.02 bits or CI crosses zero.

tests_run.test: partition corpus into currier a (11,022 words) and currier b (23,766 words) tokens. compute per-glyp
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: A measurable, statistically significant entropy difference between Currier A and B at the glyph level, in the predicted direction, would confirm that the two scribes used cipher tables with different homophone densities â€” enabling separate cryptanalysis of each sub-corpus independently and doubling decipherment leverage.
